score_explanations.revenue_concentration: High concentration risk. Established brands (Shark, roborock, iRobot, Eureka) dominate with 60%+ of visible review volume. score_explanations.capital_efficiency: Poor capital efficiency. Robot vacuums are heavy, bulky, and complex. Estimated per-unit costs: COGS $90-150, China shipping $12-18/unit (dimensional weight), Amazon FBA inbound $8-12/unit, packaging $8-10, buffer inventory. To achieve meaningful revenue ($10K/month at 10-unit weekly sales), you need 50+ units in rotation, consuming $8K-12K just in inventory. Additionally, FBA fees for bulky goods eat 35%+ of sale price. A $300 robot vacuum nets only ~$85 gross margin after all fees, requiring high volume to justify operational costs.
score_explanations.barrier_to_entry: Extremely high. Requirements: (1) FCC certification (required for WiFi-enabled devices), CE marking (EU), UL for electrical components—adds 3-6 months and $15K-25K in compliance costs. (2) Complex supply chain: must source reliable motors, sensors, LiDAR modules, self-emptying base engineering. (3) China manufacturing MOQ is typically 500-1000+ units (non-negotiable for custom models). (4) Intense brand competition from Shark, iRobot, roborock, and emerging Chinese OEMs with established supply chains. (5) Requires significant R&D for competitive features (obstacle avoidance, mop integration, tangling prevention). (6) Warranty/support infrastructure essential (product failure rate ~5-8% in first year). New entrants face 12-18 month development cycle before first sale.
